Chapter 8: MNH Resource Allocation

Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-12

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Frame a multi-country resource allocation problem as a decision under uncertainty
  • Build probability distributions over government co-financing, intervention coverage, and health outcomes
  • Apply sequential decision making (commit → observe → scale) with backward induction
  • Assess stakeholder response (crowding out vs. crowding in) as a strategic variable
  • Use Monte Carlo simulation in R to compare allocation strategies and quantify confidence intervals

Book Context: HaHCo

Resource allocation under uncertainty — the original case

The HaHCo Problem

HaHCo is a ride-sharing company deciding how to allocate drivers across cities (San Francisco vs. New York). The key uncertainties: market demand in each city, competitive dynamics, and the legal/regulatory response from incumbent taxi companies.

Decision: How many drivers to deploy in SF vs. NY?

Uncertainties:

  • Market size in each city
  • Competitor legal response
  • Regulatory environment

Core tradeoff:

  • Concentrate in one city → higher payoff if that market is large, but riskier
  • Spread across cities → lower variance, potentially lower peak return

From HaHCo to the Beginnings Fund

The same analytical framework applies to global health resource allocation:

HaHCo (Book) Beginnings Fund (MNH)
Drivers across cities $525M across 10 countries
Market demand uncertainty Baseline burden + intervention coverage uncertainty
Competitor legal response Government co-financing commitment
Regulatory environment Health system readiness
Revenue maximization Lives saved maximization

Same structure, different domain. The tools from the book — influence diagrams, expected value, decision trees, and sensitivity analysis — apply directly to the Beginnings Fund’s allocation problem.

The Multi-Country Allocation Problem

$525M across 10 countries over 76 months

The Beginnings Fund Portfolio

The Fund: $525M committed over 76 months to reduce maternal and neonatal mortality across sub-Saharan Africa. The Investment Committee must decide how much to allocate to which countries and when.

Country Lives Saved Target Phase Baseline NMR (per 1,000)
Ethiopia 86,124 Phase 1 (2025) 28.1
Tanzania 63,497 Phase 1 (2025) 20.3
Kenya 46,494 Phase 1 (2025) 19.6
Uganda 43,058 Phase 2 (2026) 19.0
Ghana 23,910 Phase 1 (2025) 24.5
Malawi 20,761 Phase 1 (2025) 22.4
Zimbabwe 20,082 Phase 1 (2025) 23.1
Rwanda 14,585 Phase 1 (2025) 16.2
Lesotho 4,336 Phase 2 (2026) 34.8
Nigeria TBD Fund Two (2028) 35.9
Total 322,847

What Makes This Hard

Each country differs on three critical dimensions:

Baseline Burden

The number of maternal and neonatal deaths preventable with known interventions. Ethiopia has the largest target (86,124 lives) because of both high NMR and large birth cohort.

Health System Capacity

Can the country absorb and deploy interventions effectively? Rwanda (NMR 16.2) has strong systems; Lesotho (NMR 34.8) has weaker infrastructure but high need per capita.

Government Readiness

Will the government maintain co-financing commitments, sustain policy reforms, and absorb programs after the Fund exits? This is the most uncertain variable — and the one with the largest impact on long-run outcomes.

Sources of Uncertainty

Key Insight: The Fund cannot simply allocate proportional to burden. Each dollar’s impact depends on uncertain, country-specific factors that interact with the allocation decision.

Uncertainty Description Impact
Government co-financing Will governments sustain funding after Fund exits? Determines long-run impact
Intervention coverage Will programs reach target populations? Determines short-run lives saved
Workforce retention Will trained health workers stay in post? Determines capacity sustainability
Political stability Will elections or conflict disrupt programs? Binary risk (derails everything)
Other donor behavior Will other donors crowd in or crowd out? Changes marginal value of Fund $

The fundamental challenge: We must commit funds before resolving these uncertainties. The question is not “What is the right allocation?” but “What allocation is robust to the uncertainty we face?”

The Allocation Decision Space

The Fund must choose an allocation vector: \(\mathbf{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{10})\) where \(a_i\) = dollars to country \(i\) and \(\sum a_i = \$525M\).

Three archetype strategies:

Concentrate

Allocate 70%+ to the top 3 countries by expected lives saved (Ethiopia, Tanzania, Kenya).

High expected value, high variance.

Spread

Allocate roughly equally across all 10 countries.

Lower expected value, lower variance.

Tiered

Weight by burden × capacity score, with floors and ceilings per country.

Balanced approach.

This is the HaHCo problem at scale. Instead of 2 cities, we have 10 countries. Instead of drivers, we have $525M. The analytical approach is the same: model uncertainty, compute expected outcomes, and find the robust allocation.

Modeling Uncertainty

Probability distributions over key variables

Government Co-Financing Scenarios

Government co-financing is the probability that the government will maintain or increase its health budget commitments after the Fund’s investment period ends. This is the single most important uncertainty.

Scenario Probability Description Impact on Lives Saved
High 0.40 Government meets or exceeds commitments; absorbs programs 100% of target achieved
Medium 0.50 Partial commitment; some programs sustained 60% of target achieved
Low 0.10 Government reneges; programs collapse post-exit 25% of target achieved

Expected achievement rate: \(0.40 \times 1.00 + 0.50 \times 0.60 + 0.10 \times 0.25 = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.025 = \mathbf{0.725}\) (72.5% of target)

This is the base case expected outcome for any country. But co-financing probabilities vary by country.

Country-Specific Probability Profiles

Not all countries carry the same risk profile:

Country P(High) P(Medium) P(Low) Expected Achievement
Rwanda 0.60 0.35 0.05 83.3%
Kenya 0.50 0.40 0.10 76.5%
Ethiopia 0.40 0.45 0.15 70.8%
Tanzania 0.45 0.40 0.15 72.8%
Ghana 0.35 0.50 0.15 68.3%
Malawi 0.30 0.50 0.20 65.0%
Uganda 0.35 0.45 0.20 67.0%
Zimbabwe 0.25 0.45 0.30 59.5%
Lesotho 0.20 0.45 0.35 55.8%
Nigeria 0.30 0.40 0.30 61.5%

Key pattern: Countries with stronger health systems (Rwanda, Kenya) have higher expected achievement. But the countries with the largest burden (Ethiopia, Nigeria) carry more uncertainty. This creates the core allocation tradeoff.

Decision Tree for a Single Country

Consider allocating $50M to Ethiopia:

Allocate
$50M to ETH
→
Gov’t
Co-finance
→
High (0.40): 86,124 lives
Med (0.45): 51,674 lives
Low (0.15): 21,531 lives
→
EV =
60,932

\(EV(\text{Ethiopia}) = 0.40 \times 86{,}124 + 0.45 \times 51{,}674 + 0.15 \times 21{,}531 = 34{,}450 + 23{,}253 + 3{,}230 = \mathbf{60{,}932}\) lives

But this assumes the full $50M goes to Ethiopia. The real question is: would those dollars save more lives if allocated to Kenya or Tanzania instead? We need to compare expected lives saved per dollar across countries.

Scenario Analysis: Best / Base / Worst

Across the full portfolio ($525M, all 10 countries):

Scenario Assumption Total Lives Saved % of Target
Best Case All countries achieve High co-financing 322,847 100%
Base Case Country-specific probability mix 228,417 70.7%
Worst Case All countries achieve Low co-financing 80,712 25.0%

Base case is credible. The weighted-average expected outcome delivers ~70% of the target — consistent with typical large-scale global health programs.

Worst case is devastating. If co-financing fails across the board, only 80,712 of 322,847 targeted lives would be saved. This tail risk motivates portfolio diversification.

Sequential Decision Making

Commit → Observe → Scale

The Multi-Stage Structure

The Fund does not have to commit all $525M on day one. Phased rollout enables sequential decision making:

Stage 1
Commit seed
funding ($50M)
→
Observe
Pilot results
(12–18 months)
→
Stage 2
Scale or
reallocate
→
Outcome
Lives saved
(76 months)

This is exactly the HaHCo structure. The book’s ride-sharing company can test a market before scaling. The Beginnings Fund can run pilots before committing full allocations. The value of this optionality depends on how informative the pilot results are.

Rolling Back the Decision Tree

Backward induction for a 2-stage Ethiopia decision:

Stage 2 (after observing pilot):

Pilot Signal Updated P(High) Optimal Stage 2 Expected Lives
Promising 0.70 Scale up ($100M) 72,804
Mixed 0.35 Moderate ($60M) 48,203
Negative 0.10 Redirect funds 12,156

Stage 1 (before pilot): P(Promising) = 0.35, P(Mixed) = 0.45, P(Negative) = 0.20

\(EV(\text{Sequential}) = 0.35 \times 72{,}804 + 0.45 \times 48{,}203 + 0.20 \times 12{,}156 = 25{,}481 + 21{,}691 + 2{,}431 = \mathbf{49{,}604}\)

Compare to commit-all-upfront EV of 60,932 lives. The sequential approach yields fewer expected lives (49,604) because less money is committed. But the key advantage is risk reduction — the worst case is much better.

The Value of Waiting vs. the Cost of Delay

Value of waiting:

  • Better information → better allocation
  • Avoid committing $100M to a country with Low co-financing
  • EVPI from Chapter 7 applies: pilot provides imperfect but valuable signal
  • Reduces portfolio variance

Cost of delay:

  • Lives lost during pilot period. Every month of delay = preventable deaths occurring
  • Roughly 4,500 neonatal deaths per month across the 10 countries that could be averted
  • 18-month pilot delay ≈ 81,000 potential lives at stake
  • Staff and political momentum may dissipate

The cost of delay is measured in lives. Sequential decision making is valuable, but not free. The Fund must balance the information value of waiting against the human cost of inaction. This is a distinctive feature of global health versus commercial allocation problems.

MNH Application: 2-Stage Decision for Nigeria

Nigeria Allocation Decision

Context: Nigeria is planned for Fund Two (2028). The Investment Committee must decide now whether to begin preparatory investments.

Stage 1 (2026): Invest $15M in a health system strengthening pilot in 3 Nigerian states.

Observe (2027): Government co-financing behavior, workforce absorption, political stability.

Stage 2 (2028): Based on pilot results, commit $0–$120M from Fund Two.

Decision Path P Investment Expected Lives
Pilot → Promising → Full scale 0.30 $135M 48,000
Pilot → Mixed → Partial scale 0.40 $55M 18,000
Pilot → Negative → Exit 0.30 $15M 2,000
EV(Sequential) $62M 22,400
No pilot → Full commit 1.00 $120M 25,000

The pilot saves $58M in expected cost ($62M vs. $120M) while sacrificing only 2,600 expected lives (22,400 vs. 25,000). The freed capital can be deployed in higher-confidence countries.

Stakeholder Response

Other donors and governments as strategic actors

Crowding Out vs. Crowding In

Crowding Out: When the Fund’s investment causes other donors or the government to reduce their own spending on MNH — “the Fund will cover it.”

Crowding In: When the Fund’s investment causes others to increase their spending — “this sector is getting results; let’s invest more.”

Crowding Out Scenario

Fund invests $50M in Tanzania MNH.

Government redirects $20M from MNH to other priorities.

Net new investment: $30M.

Impact: 63% of expected lives saved.

Crowding In Scenario

Fund invests $50M in Tanzania MNH.

Government adds $15M in matching funds. World Bank adds $10M.

Net new investment: $75M.

Impact: 150% of expected lives saved.

Modeling Other Donors

Other donors (World Bank, Global Fund, USAID, bilateral agencies) respond to the Fund’s allocation:

Fund Action Other Donor Response P Net Multiplier
Large investment in country X Redirect away (“covered”) 0.30 0.7×
Large investment in country X Maintain existing programs 0.45 1.0×
Large investment in country X Match/expand programs 0.25 1.4×

\(\text{Expected multiplier} = 0.30 \times 0.7 + 0.45 \times 1.0 + 0.25 \times 1.4 = 0.21 + 0.45 + 0.35 = \mathbf{1.01}\)

On average, the multiplier is roughly 1.0 — crowding out and crowding in approximately cancel. But the variance matters: in 30% of cases the Fund’s dollar buys only $0.70 of new investment. Strategic coordination with other donors can shift these probabilities.

Preview: Game Theory (Chapter 9)

The stakeholder response problem is fundamentally a game theory problem. Each actor (Fund, government, other donors) makes decisions considering what the others will do.

In Chapter 9, we will formalize this with:

  • Nash equilibrium — stable allocation patterns where no actor wants to deviate
  • Commitment devices — co-financing agreements that bind government behavior
  • Signaling — how the Fund’s early investments signal priorities to other donors
  • Mechanism design — structuring incentives so all actors align toward lives saved

For now: We treat stakeholder response as an uncertain variable in our decision model (a chance node). In Chapter 9, we upgrade it to a strategic variable with its own decision logic.

MNH Application: 3-Country Allocation

Full decision analysis for Ethiopia, Kenya, and Tanzania

The 3-Country Problem

Simplified scenario: The Investment Committee has $200M to allocate across Ethiopia, Kenya, and Tanzania — the three largest target countries. How should it split the funds?

Country Lives Saved Target Expected Achievement Lives per $M
Ethiopia 86,124 70.8% 610
Kenya 46,494 76.5% 356
Tanzania 63,497 72.8% 462

Naive approach: Allocate proportional to lives-per-dollar → send almost everything to Ethiopia. But this ignores risk (Ethiopia has more uncertainty) and diminishing returns (marginal impact decreases with scale).

Influence Diagram for 3-Country Allocation

Allocation
\(a_{ETH}, a_{KEN}, a_{TZA}\)
→
Coverage
(per country)
→
Total
Lives Saved
↑       ↑
Gov’t Co-
financing
Donor
Response
  • Decision node (rectangle): Allocation vector \((a_{ETH}, a_{KEN}, a_{TZA})\) with \(\sum = \$200M\)
  • Chance nodes (ovals): Coverage per country, influenced by allocation and uncertain factors
  • Uncertain variables (amber): Government co-financing and other donor response — both country-specific
  • Objective (hexagon): Total lives saved across all three countries

Comparing Strategies

Three allocation strategies for $200M across Ethiopia/Kenya/Tanzania:

Strategy Ethiopia Kenya Tanzania Expected Lives Std Dev
Concentrate $140M $30M $30M 112,460 28,400
Spread $67M $67M $67M 104,280 19,100
Tiered $90M $45M $65M 109,850 22,600

Concentrate has the highest expected value (112,460) but also the highest risk (std dev 28,400). A bad outcome in Ethiopia is catastrophic.

Tiered sacrifices 2,610 expected lives (2.3%) relative to Concentrate but reduces standard deviation by 20%. This is the efficient frontier logic from portfolio theory.

Sensitivity to Government Co-Financing

At what P(High co-financing) does Concentrate beat Tiered?

Let \(p\) = P(High co-financing) for Ethiopia specifically:

\(EV(\text{Concentrate}) = 140M \times [p \times 610 + (1-p) \times 280] / M = 140 \times (280 + 330p)\)

\(EV(\text{Tiered}) = 90 \times (280 + 330p) + 45 \times (190 + 166p) + 65 \times (250 + 212p)\)

Ethiopia P(High) EV(Concentrate) EV(Tiered) Best Strategy
0.20 93,800 97,250 Tiered
0.40 112,460 109,850 Concentrate
0.60 131,120 122,450 Concentrate

Crossover point: P(High) ≈ 0.35. If the Investment Committee believes Ethiopia has a >35% chance of high co-financing, concentrating funds becomes optimal in expectation. But the risk-adjusted answer depends on the Committee’s risk tolerance.

Recommendation with Confidence Intervals

Recommended allocation: Tiered strategy

Ethiopia $90M | Kenya $45M | Tanzania $65M

Expected lives saved: 109,850 (95% CI: 68,300 – 148,200)

Rationale

  1. Robustness: Tiered performs within 3% of Concentrate under favorable conditions and substantially better under adverse conditions (worst-case gap: +14,200 lives)

  2. Portfolio diversification: Spreading across 3 countries with different risk profiles reduces the probability of catastrophic shortfall

  3. Political feasibility: Extreme concentration raises equity concerns among participating governments

  4. Adaptive capacity: Tiered allocation preserves the option to reallocate in Phase 2 based on observed performance

R Code Workshop

Monte Carlo simulation and optimal allocation in R

R Block 1: Monte Carlo Simulation

set.seed(42)
n_sims <- 10000

# Country parameters: target lives, expected lives per $M, sd per $M
countries <- data.frame(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Tanzania"),
  target = c(86124, 46494, 63497),
  lives_per_M = c(610, 356, 462),
  sd_per_M = c(185, 95, 130),  # uncertainty in effectiveness
  p_high = c(0.40, 0.50, 0.45)  # P(high gov co-financing)
)

# Simulate outcomes for a given allocation
simulate_outcomes <- function(alloc, countries, n_sims) {
  total_lives <- numeric(n_sims)
  for (i in 1:n_sims) {
    lives <- 0
    for (j in 1:nrow(countries)) {
      # Government co-financing scenario
      gov <- sample(c("High", "Med", "Low"), 1,
                    prob = c(countries$p_high[j],
                             1 - countries$p_high[j] - 0.10,
                             0.10))
      multiplier <- switch(gov, High = 1.0, Med = 0.6, Low = 0.25)
      # Lives saved = allocation * lives per $M * multiplier + noise
      lives_j <- alloc[j] * rnorm(1, countries$lives_per_M[j],
                                      countries$sd_per_M[j]) * multiplier
      lives <- lives + max(0, lives_j)
    }
    total_lives[i] <- lives
  }
  total_lives
}

# Three strategies (in $M)
strat_concentrate <- c(140, 30, 30)  # Heavy Ethiopia
strat_spread      <- c(67, 67, 67)   # Equal
strat_tiered      <- c(90, 45, 65)   # Weighted

R Block 2: Visualize Strategy Outcomes

library(ggplot2)

# Run simulations
sim_conc   <- simulate_outcomes(strat_concentrate, countries, n_sims)
sim_spread <- simulate_outcomes(strat_spread, countries, n_sims)
sim_tiered <- simulate_outcomes(strat_tiered, countries, n_sims)

# Combine into data frame
sim_df <- data.frame(
  lives = c(sim_conc, sim_spread, sim_tiered),
  strategy = rep(c("Concentrate", "Spread", "Tiered"),
                 each = n_sims)
)

# Violin plot with embedded box plot
ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = strategy, y = lives / 1000, fill = strategy)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, draw_quantiles = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.8, outlier.shape = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "Allocation Strategy",
       y = "Total Lives Saved (thousands)",
       title = "Simulated Outcomes: 3-Country Allocation ($200M)",
       subtitle = "10,000 Monte Carlo draws | Violin shows full distribution") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

R Block 3: Optimal Allocation via Grid Search

# Grid search over allocation space
# Constraint: a_ETH + a_KEN + a_TZA = 200 (in $M)
grid_step <- 10  # $10M increments
results <- data.frame()

for (a_eth in seq(0, 200, grid_step)) {
  for (a_ken in seq(0, 200 - a_eth, grid_step)) {
    a_tza <- 200 - a_eth - a_ken
    alloc <- c(a_eth, a_ken, a_tza)

    # Quick simulation (fewer draws for speed)
    sim <- simulate_outcomes(alloc, countries, n_sims = 2000)

    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
      a_eth = a_eth, a_ken = a_ken, a_tza = a_tza,
      ev = mean(sim),
      sd = sd(sim),
      p5 = quantile(sim, 0.05)  # 5th percentile (worst case)
    ))
  }
}

# Best by expected value
best_ev <- results[which.max(results$ev), ]
cat("Max EV allocation:", best_ev$a_eth, "/",
    best_ev$a_ken, "/", best_ev$a_tza, "\n")
cat("Expected lives:", round(best_ev$ev), "\n")

# Best by worst-case (maximin)
best_p5 <- results[which.max(results$p5), ]
cat("\nMaximin allocation:", best_p5$a_eth, "/",
    best_p5$a_ken, "/", best_p5$a_tza, "\n")
cat("5th percentile lives:", round(best_p5$p5), "\n")

R Block 4: Sensitivity Analysis

library(tidyr)

# Sensitivity: vary Ethiopia P(High co-financing) from 0.1 to 0.8
p_seq <- seq(0.1, 0.8, by = 0.05)
sens_results <- data.frame()

for (p_high_eth in p_seq) {
  # Update Ethiopia's co-financing probability
  countries_mod <- countries
  countries_mod$p_high[1] <- p_high_eth

  ev_conc <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_concentrate, countries_mod, 3000))
  ev_spr  <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_spread, countries_mod, 3000))
  ev_tier <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_tiered, countries_mod, 3000))

  sens_results <- rbind(sens_results, data.frame(
    p_high = p_high_eth,
    Concentrate = ev_conc,
    Spread = ev_spr,
    Tiered = ev_tier
  ))
}

# Pivot to long format and plot
sens_long <- pivot_longer(sens_results, cols = -p_high,
                          names_to = "Strategy", values_to = "EV")

ggplot(sens_long, aes(x = p_high, y = EV / 1000, color = Strategy)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.40, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "P(High Gov't Co-financing) for Ethiopia",
       y = "Expected Lives Saved (thousands)",
       title = "Sensitivity: How Ethiopia's Co-financing Risk Affects Strategy Choice",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = current estimate (p = 0.40)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

Key Takeaways

Resource allocation under uncertainty requires balancing expected impact against risk. The optimal allocation depends not just on where lives can be saved most efficiently, but on how uncertain we are about each country’s trajectory.

  • Portfolio thinking applies to global health: diversification reduces catastrophic shortfall risk
  • Sequential decisions (pilot → scale) add value by resolving uncertainty — but delay costs lives
  • Government co-financing is the dominant uncertainty; small shifts in P(High) change the optimal strategy
  • Stakeholder response (crowding out/in) can amplify or undermine the Fund’s investment
  • Monte Carlo simulation in R makes it possible to quantify confidence intervals and compare strategies rigorously
  • The cost of delay is measured in lives — a distinctive feature of health versus commercial allocation

Looking Ahead

Next: Chapter 9 — Multi-Agent Decision Making and Game Theory

We move from treating stakeholders as uncertain variables to modeling them as strategic agents:

  • Nash equilibrium in donor coordination games
  • Commitment devices that bind government co-financing
  • Mechanism design for aligning incentives across funders
  • MNH application: Designing a co-financing framework that maximizes collective impact

From Ch 8 to Ch 9: We solved the allocation problem as if government and donor behavior were exogenous uncertainties. In Chapter 9, we recognize these actors have their own objectives — and we design strategies that account for their rational self-interest.





Inference and Intervention | Chapter 8: MNH Resource Allocation

BK Advisors
